mechanism_narrative: A falling FEDFUNDS path lowers the discount rate on equities and lifts risk appetite, so a clear easing trend should produce positive S&P 500 returns over the next month. The signal is falsified if rate cuts coincide with weak equity performance or if the effect disappears once inflation is not accelerating.
failure_condition: If monthly declines in FEDFUNDS are not followed by above-baseline 20d SPX returns, or if triggered easing episodes produce negative returns in more than 55% of cases, reject the hypothesis.

mechanism_narrative: When FEDFUNDS is above a restrictive threshold, financing conditions are tight and equity valuations compress, so forward SPX returns over the next month should be negative. The signal is strongest when inflation is still elevated, because real rates are then even more restrictive.
failure_condition: Reject if periods with FEDFUNDS above 4.5% do not show negative average 20d SPX returns, or if adding CPIAUCSL does not improve discrimination versus FEDFUNDS alone.

mechanism_narrative: A rising UNRATE signals deteriorating labor income and weaker consumer demand, which should depress S&P 500 returns over the next month. The effect should appear when unemployment rises faster than the recent trend, not when it is flat.
failure_condition: Reject if UNRATE acceleration events fail to precede negative 20d SPX returns, or if their average forward return is not below the unconditional mean by at least 1 standard error.

mechanism_narrative: Payroll growth combined with a falling unemployment rate marks a broadening expansion in household income, which should support positive S&P 500 returns over the next quarter. The signal fails when payrolls rise but unemployment also rises, because that combination indicates stagnant labor quality rather than healthy demand.
failure_condition: Reject if joint PAYEMS gains and UNRATE declines do not outperform single-series signals, or if positive 60d returns are not more frequent than negative returns in those joint expansion periods.

mechanism_narrative: Retail sales growth reflects consumer spending momentum, so a month-over-month rise in RSAFS should predict positive S&P 500 returns over the next week as revenue expectations are revised upward. The relationship should weaken if sales gains are entirely offset by higher rates or deteriorating labor data.
failure_condition: Reject if RSAFS monthly increases do not lead to above-zero 5d SPX returns more often than not, or if the sign of the average return is non-positive across triggered observations.

mechanism_narrative: A surge in housing starts captures easier credit and stronger cyclical demand, which should support S&P 500 returns over the following month. The signal is weaker when the housing increase is already large but broad manufacturing activity is rolling over.
failure_condition: Reject if housing-start surges fail to coincide with positive 20d SPX returns, or if the hit rate is not meaningfully above 50% after the trigger fires.

mechanism_narrative: Rising INDPRO together with stable employment indicates genuine supply-side output growth rather than inflationary overheating, which should support positive equity returns over the next quarter. If industrial production rises while unemployment also rises, the mechanism fails because production gains are not being transmitted into broad earnings strength.
failure_condition: Reject if joint INDPRO strength and UNRATE stability do not produce positive 60d SPX returns, or if the combined signal underperforms either proxy alone in out-of-sample tests.

mechanism_narrative: During a post-shock recovery, concurrent improvement in GDPC1 and INDPRO indicates synchronized demand and supply healing, which should generate positive S&P 500 returns over the next month. The hypothesis is falsified if growth in GDP does not coincide with industrial improvement or if the equity response flips negative in recovery phases.
failure_condition: Reject if periods with concurrent GDPC1 and INDPRO improvement do not deliver positive 20d SPX returns, or if the interaction term is not statistically better than either series alone.
